risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After completing the baseline assessment, the research nurse telephoned a secure and remote randomisation service, located at the York Trials Unit (University of York, UK). Randomisation was conducted using pre-generated random permuted blocks (block sizes of four and six). A data manager at the York Trials Unit, who was completely independent of the research team, created the randomisation programme, which was used to allocate participants to one of the two treatment arms" Comment: The study explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method using pre-generated random permuted blocks and an independently created randomisation programme, which supports truly random allocation.

Quote: "After completing the baseline assessment, the research nurse telephoned a secure and remote randomisation service, located at the York Trials Unit (University of York, UK)." and "A data manager at the York Trials Unit, who was completely independent of the research team, created the randomisation programme..." Comment: This is an explicit central/remote randomisation mechanism run independently of the recruiting team, which would prevent the person enrolling participants from foreseeing the upcoming allocation before enrolment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Since assessment of wound healing is somewhat subjective and staff and participants could not be blinded to treatment, it would be important to conduct blinded outcome assessment in a full trial." Comment: The paper explicitly states that participants and staff were not blinded. This trial also collected outcomes that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation, including ulcer-related pain (VAS), health-related quality of life (EQ-5D), and clinically judged healing/adverse-event related care decisions. Therefore lack of blinding is likely to introduce performance bias.

Quote: "Whilst the research nurse was primarily responsible for data collection, this responsibility was also delegated to nurses treating patients in acute and community settings." "Since assessment of wound healing is somewhat subjective and staff and participants could not be blinded to treatment, it would be important to conduct blinded outcome assessment in a full trial." "At baseline, patients were also asked how intense the pain from their pressure ulcer(s) had been in the previous 24 h... The patient’s treatment preference was recorded and participants were also asked to complete the EQ-5D health-related quality of life (HR-QoL) questionnaire." Comment: Outcome assessors included patient self-report for pain and EQ-5D, and clinicians/research nurses for some clinical outcomes/adverse events. Although healing photographs were reviewed by a blinded independent assessor, the study explicitly states that staff and participants could not be blinded to treatment. Because the study included subjective patient-reported outcomes (pain, HR-QoL), the patient was the assessor and was unblinded; under the worst-outcome rule this makes the domain high risk overall, even though blinded assessment was piloted for healing photographs.

Quote: "There was no attrition recorded during this trial. From the 12 recruited participants, 5 (2 NPWT and 3 SC participants) exited the trial after completing the maximum 6-month follow-up. A further five participants (two NPWT and three SC) completed less than the 6-month follow-up as they were recruited later into the study. Two participants died during the trial..." Comment: Post-randomization follow-up on the trial outcomes was essentially complete: all 12 randomised participants were accounted for (6 NPWT, 6 SC), with no losses to follow-up or withdrawals reported. The five who had shorter follow-up were shorter by design because they were recruited later, not because of missing outcome data. Two deaths occurred after randomization, but these were reported as observed outcomes/events rather than unexplained attrition. On the reported trial outcomes, there is positive evidence of complete/balanced follow-up and no meaningful attrition bias.
